slides.mascot_explanation: Now for the fun part - squeezing! Use your strong muscles to twist and press down. Watch how the juice comes out drop by drop into our cup!

items: Can you show me how to roll the orange to soften it?
What do we do with the orange after we cut it in half?
Why do we wash our hands before making food?
slides.mascot_explanation: Let's make sure we remember all the important steps for making juice! You've become such good juice makers today.

slides.text: Pour juice into cups for tasting, clean all materials, and store any extra juice in refrigerator. Discuss how the orange changed from whole fruit to delicious juice.
slides.mastery_check: Child can squeeze orange halves independently and explain the basic process of making fresh juice.
slides.mascot_explanation: Time to taste our amazing fresh juice! Doesn't it taste so much better when you make it yourself? You should feel proud of all your hard work today.

title: Moveable Alphabet CVC Words: Building HEN, SIT, MOP
description: Children use the Moveable Alphabet to construct three-letter consonant-vowel-consonant words, focusing on 'hen', 'sit', and 'mop'. This hands-on approach helps bridge the gap between phonetic knowledge and reading comprehension.
instructions: Set up Moveable Alphabet box with red vowels and blue consonants clearly separated.
Review the three target pictures: hen, sit, mop, saying each word slowly to hear all sounds.
Start with 'hen' - have child find and place h, then e, then n in sequence.
Encourage child to sound out each letter as they place it, then blend the whole word.
Move to 'sit' following the same process of finding letters and sounding out.
Complete with 'mop', allowing child to work more independently if ready.
Practice reading each completed word by pointing and blending sounds together.
Store letters back in proper compartments when finished.
materials_needed: Picture cards for hen, sit, mop
parent_notes: The Moveable Alphabet is a foundational Montessori material that allows children to build words before they can write them fluently. This concrete manipulation of letters helps children understand that words are made up of individual sounds in sequence. Start with words the child can decode phonetically, building confidence before introducing sight words. The physical act of selecting and placing letters reinforces the connection between sounds and symbols, preparing for both reading and writing.

slides.heading: Moveable Alphabet CVC Words: Building HEN, SIT, MOP
slides.subheading: Using letters to build three-letter words
slides.mascot_explanation: Hello word builders! I'm Betsy Bear and I love making words with letters! Today we're going to build three special words using our moveable alphabet - it's like playing with letter puzzle pieces!

items: Picture cards for hen, sit, mop
slides.setup_instructions: Place moveable alphabet box at top of workspace with picture cards arranged in order below.
slides.mascot_explanation: Look at our special letter box! The red letters are vowels and the blue letters are consonants. We also have picture cards to help us remember what words we're building today.

slides.text: Look at the picture of 'hen' and say the word slowly: h-e-n. Listen for each sound.
slides.mascot_explanation: Let's start with our feathered friend the hen! When we say 'hen' really slowly, we can hear three different sounds. Can you hear them with your bear ears?

slides.text: Find the letter 'h' from the consonants and place it first on your mat. Then find 'e' from the vowels, and finally 'n'.
slides.mascot_explanation: Now we're letter detectives! First we find the 'h' sound, then the 'e' in the middle, and then 'n' at the end. Line them up like a little train!

slides.prompt: Build 'sit' and 'mop' using the same process. Sound out each letter as you place it, then blend the sounds together to read the word.
slides.mascot_explanation: You're doing such great work! Now try 'sit' and 'mop' the same way. Remember to say each sound as you find the letters, then put them all together to make the word!

items: Can you sound out h-e-n and blend it together?
Which letters are vowels in our words today?
How many sounds do you hear in the word 'mop'?
slides.mascot_explanation: Let's check how well you know your word building! You've become such a good letter detective today. I'm so proud of your hard work!

slides.text: Practice reading all three completed words by pointing to each letter and blending sounds. Return letters to proper compartments in the alphabet box.
slides.mastery_check: Child can independently select correct letters for CVC words and blend sounds to read the completed words.
slides.mascot_explanation: What amazing word builders you are! You made three whole words with just letters. Now let's put our letters back in their special homes so they're ready for more word building adventures!

title: Hundred Board: Finding Pattern of Tens (10, 20, 30, 40, 50)
description: Children explore the Hundred Board by identifying and working with multiples of ten up to 50. This activity reinforces place value understanding and prepares children for skip counting patterns.
instructions: Set up Hundred Board with control chart and number tiles 1-50 available.
Review counting by ones up to 20 to warm up number recognition.
Point out the pattern in the rightmost column: 10, 20, 30, 40, 50 and have child place these tiles.
Count together by tens while pointing to each tile: 'Ten, twenty, thirty, forty, fifty!'
Remove the tens tiles and have child replace them independently.
Explore what comes before and after each multiple of ten using adjacent tiles.
Practice skip counting by tens starting from different numbers if child is ready.
Clean up by returning tiles to proper order in the box.
parent_notes: The Hundred Board is a key Montessori material for developing number sense and understanding of our decimal system. Working with multiples of ten helps children see patterns in our number system and prepares them for more complex mathematical operations. The visual and tactile nature of placing tiles reinforces learning more effectively than abstract worksheets. Allow children to discover patterns naturally rather than forcing memorization, as understanding the 'why' behind patterns is more valuable than rote learning.

slides.heading: Hundred Board: Finding Pattern of Tens (10, 20, 30, 40, 50)
slides.subheading: Discovering the special pattern of counting by tens
slides.mascot_explanation: Hi number explorers! I'm Betsy Bear and I love finding patterns! Today we're going to discover something really cool about numbers that end in zero on our Hundred Board.

slides.setup_instructions: Place Hundred Board on workspace with number tiles organized numerically nearby and control chart available for reference.
slides.mascot_explanation: Here's our amazing Hundred Board! It's like a number neighborhood where every number has its own special house. We'll use these number tiles to find a super cool pattern today!

slides.text: Count from 1 to 20 to warm up, then look for all the numbers that end in zero: 10, 20, 30, 40, 50.
slides.mascot_explanation: Let's wake up our counting voices first! Now, can you spot the special numbers that end in zero? They're like the captains of each row!

slides.text: Place the tiles 10, 20, 30, 40, 50 in their correct positions on the board. Notice they make a straight line down the right side.
slides.mascot_explanation: Look at that! When we put 10, 20, 30, 40, 50 in their homes, they make a perfect line going down. It's like they're all friends standing in a row!

slides.prompt: Practice skip counting by tens: 'Ten, twenty, thirty, forty, fifty!' Point to each tile as you say it. Remove tiles and place them again independently.
slides.mascot_explanation: Now for the fun part - let's count by jumps of ten! It's like taking big bear steps through the numbers. Can you do it all by yourself now?

items: Can you place the tens tiles (10, 20, 30, 40, 50) in the right spots?
Can you count by tens from 10 to 50?
slides.mascot_explanation: Let's see how well you know your tens pattern! You've been such a good number detective today. Show me what you've learned!

slides.text: Count by tens one more time while pointing to each tile. Discuss how tens make a pattern and return all tiles to the box in numerical order.
slides.mastery_check: Child can independently identify and place multiples of ten up to 50 and demonstrate skip counting by tens.
slides.mascot_explanation: Wow! You discovered the amazing pattern of tens! Numbers that end in zero are really special - they help us count faster and see how numbers work together. Great job today!

title: Montessori Bells: Matching Pairs C-D-E
description: Children explore the first three pairs of Montessori bells, learning to match identical pitches by careful listening. This foundational music work develops auditory discrimination and appreciation for musical tones.
instructions: Set up three brown bells (C, D, E) on left side and three white bells with same pitches mixed up on right.
Demonstrate proper bell handling: hold by wooden handle, strike gently with mallet.
Strike the first brown bell (C) and listen to the sound together, letting it fade completely.
Strike each white bell until child identifies the matching sound to pair with brown C bell.
Repeat process with brown D bell, finding its white partner through careful listening.
Complete the activity by matching the E bells, encouraging independent listening.
Practice striking matched pairs together to hear how identical pitches sound the same.
Return bells to proper positions and discuss how sounds can be the same or different.
parent_notes: The Montessori bells provide a concrete introduction to musical concepts through sensorial exploration. This matching activity develops auditory discrimination skills that support both musical development and language phonics work. Children naturally enjoy making music, and this structured approach allows them to discover musical relationships through their own exploration. The key is to encourage careful listening rather than rushing through the activity, as the ear needs time to process and compare sounds.

slides.heading: Montessori Bells: Matching Pairs C-D-E
slides.subheading: Finding bells that sound exactly the same
slides.mascot_explanation: Hello music makers! I'm Betsy Bear and I love beautiful sounds! Today we're going to play a special listening game with these magical bells to find the ones that sound exactly the same.

slides.setup_instructions: Place brown bells in a row on left felt mat and white bells mixed up on right felt mat with mallet nearby.
slides.mascot_explanation: Look at our beautiful bells! The brown ones stay in their special places, and the white ones are all mixed up. We're going to use our super listening ears to find the matches!

slides.text: Hold the mallet gently and strike the first brown bell. Listen carefully to the sound and let it fade completely before making another sound.
slides.mascot_explanation: Let's learn the gentle way to make music with our bells! Strike softly and then listen with your bear ears as the sound gets quieter and quieter until it's gone.

slides.text: Now strike each white bell one at a time, listening for the one that sounds exactly like the brown bell you just heard.
slides.mascot_explanation: Now we become sound detectives! Try each white bell and use your amazing ears to find the one that sounds just like a twin to the brown bell.

slides.prompt: Continue matching the remaining brown and white bell pairs (D and E) by striking and listening carefully to find identical sounds.
slides.mascot_explanation: You're doing such wonderful listening! Take your time with each bell - there's no hurry in music. Let your ears tell you which bells are friends that sound the same.

items: Can you show me how to strike a bell gently?
Which white bell matches this brown bell?
Do these two bells sound the same or different?
slides.mascot_explanation: Let's check how well your ears are working today! You've become such a good listener. Show me what you've learned about matching sounds!

slides.text: Strike each matched pair together to confirm they sound identical. Return bells to starting positions and talk about how some sounds match while others are different.
slides.mastery_check: Child can independently match at least 2 out of 3 bell pairs using auditory discrimination and demonstrates proper bell handling technique.
slides.mascot_explanation: What amazing ears you have! You found all the bell friends that sound the same. Music is all around us, and now you know how to listen really carefully to beautiful sounds!

title: Chrysanthemum: Chapter 3 - Finding Confidence Again
description: In this final portion of Kevin Henkes' beloved story, children discover how Chrysanthemum regains her confidence and learns to love her unique name. This reading explores themes of self-acceptance and the power of finding allies.
instructions: Gather children comfortably for story time and briefly review what happened in previous chapters.
Show the cover again and ask children to predict how Chrysanthemum might feel better about her name.
Read Chapter 3 with expression, pausing to show illustrations and allow children to react.
Stop when Mrs. Twinkle reveals her own name to discuss how this changes everything for Chrysanthemum.
Complete the story, emphasizing how Chrysanthemum's feelings transform when she finds acceptance.
Discuss how everyone's name is special and unique, connecting to children's own experiences.
Talk about times when children have felt different and how it helped to find someone who understood.
End by having each child share something special about their own name or a friend's name.
parent_notes: This conclusion to Chrysanthemum provides a perfect opportunity to discuss themes of self-acceptance and finding allies when facing challenges. Kevin Henkes masterfully shows how one supportive person can transform a child's entire perspective. Use this story to help children understand that being different is something to celebrate, not hide. The book also opens discussions about the power of names and identity, helping children appreciate the thought that went into choosing their own names.

slides.heading: Chrysanthemum: Chapter 3 - Finding Confidence Again
slides.subheading: Discovering how Chrysanthemum learns to love her name
slides.mascot_explanation: Hello story friends! I'm Betsy Bear and I can't wait to find out how Chrysanthemum's story ends! I think something wonderful is going to happen to help her feel better about her beautiful name.

slides.setup_instructions: Arrange seating in a circle with the book prominently displayed and name cards ready for the activity after reading.
slides.mascot_explanation: Let's get cozy for our story time! We have our special book and some name cards too - I wonder what fun things we'll do with those after we finish Chrysanthemum's adventure!

slides.text: Review what happened in previous chapters: Chrysanthemum loved her name until other children made her feel bad about it.
slides.mascot_explanation: Let's remember where we left our friend Chrysanthemum. She was feeling sad about her name because some children weren't being very kind. I hope things get better for her!

slides.text: Read Chapter 3, pausing when Mrs. Twinkle reveals her full name to discuss how this surprises everyone, especially Chrysanthemum.
slides.mascot_explanation: Listen carefully to this part! Mrs. Twinkle is about to tell everyone something very special about her own name. I think this might change everything for Chrysanthemum!

slides.prompt: After finishing the story, discuss how Chrysanthemum's feelings changed and what made the difference. Talk about how everyone's name is special and unique.
slides.mascot_explanation: Now let's talk about this wonderful story! Wasn't it amazing how Mrs. Twinkle helped Chrysanthemum feel proud of her name again? Every name has a special story, just like yours does!

items: How did Chrysanthemum feel at the end of the story?
What did Mrs. Twinkle do to help Chrysanthemum?
Why is it important to be kind about people's names?
slides.mascot_explanation: Let's make sure we caught all the important parts of this beautiful story! You're such good listeners, and I love hearing your thoughts about Chrysanthemum's adventure.

slides.text: Have each child decorate their name card and share something special about their name or a friend's name. Emphasize that every name is perfect and chosen with love.
slides.mastery_check: Child can explain how Chrysanthemum's feelings changed in the story and identify what helped her feel better about her name.
slides.mascot_explanation: What a perfect ending to our story! Just like Chrysanthemum learned, every one of your names is absolutely perfect and special. I love how this story reminds us to always be kind to others!
